text: Mobile phone repair and accessories is a booming business across Uganda with 28+ million mobile subscribers. Startup: basic repair toolkit (UGX 150,000-300,000), phone parts stock — screens, batteries, chargers (UGX 300,000-500,000), small table/booth near busy area (UGX 50,000-100,000/month rent), magnifying glass (UGX 20,000). Total startup: UGX 500,000-1,000,000. Revenue: screen replacement UGX 30,000-80,000 per phone, battery replacement UGX 15,000-30,000, software flash UGX 10,000-20,000, accessory sales (covers, earphones, chargers) markup 40-60%. A busy technician can earn UGX 800,000-2,000,000/month.

Growing your phone repair business: 1) Learn from YouTube — thousands of free repair tutorials for every phone model. 2) Start with common brands (Tecno, Infinix, Samsung J-series — these are 80% of Uganda's market). 3) Buy spare parts wholesale from Kampala Road or order from Alibaba for 50% savings. 4) Add value services: data transfer, phone setup for elderly customers, SIM card registration help. 5) Partner with mobile money agents — they see customers all day who need phone help. 6) Offer 7-day warranty on repairs to build trust. 7) Consider adding second-hand phone sales (buy broken phones, fix, resell at 2-3x cost).

Location strategy for phone repair businesses: Best locations ranked by customer traffic: 1) Near main markets (Owino, Nakasero, Wandegeya, Kikuubo) — highest footfall, rent UGX 100,000-300,000/month for a small booth. 2) Near schools and universities — students constantly break screens and need accessories. Makerere, Kyambogo, UCU areas are prime spots. 3) Near taxi parks — commuters drop phones for repair and collect later. 4) Near mobile money kiosks — natural synergy with phone users. 5) Shopping malls and arcades — higher rent (UGX 200,000-500,000/month) but wealthier clientele willing to pay premium prices. Even a simple table and umbrella setup near a busy junction can work if you lack capital for rent. Key: visibility and accessibility matter more than fancy premises.

Key risks in the phone repair business: 1) Customer disputes — a phone may have pre-existing water damage that worsens during repair. Always test and document phone condition before accepting it. Take a photo of the screen with the customer present. 2) Counterfeit parts — cheap screens from unreliable suppliers fail within weeks, destroying your reputation. Build relationships with trusted wholesalers on Kampala Road. 3) Technology changes — newer phones (iPhone, Samsung Galaxy S-series) require specialised tools like heat guns and suction cups. Invest in training as phone models evolve. 4) Theft — phones left for repair are valuable targets. Keep a locked cabinet and a proper logbook with customer details. 5) Competition — the market is crowded. Differentiate by offering warranties, faster turnaround (same-day service), and phone accessories upselling. 6) Cash flow — customers sometimes abandon phones they cannot afford to repair. Set a 30-day policy and sell uncollected phones for parts.
